solution:
Each of the following functions belongs to $S$ for the reasons indicated.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ll}
$f(x), g(x)$ & given \\
$\ln(x+1)$ & (i) \\
$\ln(f(x)+1), \ln(g(x)+1)$ & (ii) plus two previous lines\\
$\ln(f(x)+1) + \ln(g(x)+1)$ & (ii) \\
$e^x - 1$ & (i) \\
$(f(x)+1)(g(x)+1) - 1$ & (ii) plus two previous lines \\
$f(x)g(x) + f(x) + g(x)$ & previous line \\
$f(x) + g(x)$ & (ii) plus first line \\
$f(x) g(x)$ & (iii) plus two previous lines
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

descriptive_long.solution:
Each of the following functions belongs to $setclass$ for the reasons indicated.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ll}
$funcone(varreal), functwo(varreal)$ & given \\
$\ln(varreal+1)$ & (i) \\
$\ln(funcone(varreal)+1), \ln(functwo(varreal)+1)$ & (ii) plus two previous lines\\
$\ln(funcone(varreal)+1) + \ln(functwo(varreal)+1)$ & (ii) \\
$e^{varreal} - 1$ & (i) \\
$(funcone(varreal)+1)(functwo(varreal)+1) - 1$ & (ii) plus two previous lines \\
$funcone(varreal)functwo(varreal) + funcone(varreal) + functwo(varreal)$ & previous line \\
$funcone(varreal) + functwo(varreal)$ & (ii) plus first line \\
$funcone(varreal) functwo(varreal)$ & (iii) plus two previous lines
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

kernel_variant.question:
Let $S$ be a collection of functions
$$f:[0,\infty)\longrightarrow[0,\infty)$$
that satisfies the following three properties.

(i)  The two functions
\[ h_1(x)=2^{x}-1\quad\text{and}\quad h_2(x)=\log_{2}(x+1) \qquad(x\ge 0) \]
are contained in $S$.

(ii)  If $f,g\in S$, then the sum $f+g$ and the composition $f\circ g$ (taken in the usual sense) are also members of $S$.

(iii)  If $f,g\in S$ and $f(x)\ge g(x)$ for every $x\ge 0$, then the difference $f-g$ belongs to $S$.

Prove that for every pair of functions $f,g\in S$, the product $f\,g$ also lies in $S$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Take arbitrary functions $f,g\in S$.  We successively manufacture the product $fg$ using only the three closure properties.

1.  First compose each of $f$ and $g$ with $h_{2}(x)=\log_{2}(x+1)$:
   \[
        A(x)=h_2(f(x))=\log_2\bigl(f(x)+1\bigr)\in S,\qquad
        B(x)=h_2(g(x))=\log_2\bigl(g(x)+1\bigr)\in S.
   \]
   (Property (ii) is used.)

2.  Add the two new members:
   \[
        C(x)=A(x)+B(x)=\log_2\bigl((f(x)+1)(g(x)+1)\bigr)\in S.
   \]
   (Property (ii) again.)

3.  Compose $C$ with $h_{1}(x)=2^{x}-1$ to obtain
   \[
        D(x)=h_{1}(C(x))=2^{C(x)}-1=(f(x)+1)(g(x)+1)-1
        =f(x)g(x)+f(x)+g(x)\in S.
   \]
   (Another application of (ii).)

4.  Observe that $D(x)\ge f(x)+g(x)$ for all $x\ge0$, because
   \[
        D(x)-(f(x)+g(x))=f(x)g(x)\ge0.
   \]
   Since $f+g\in S$ by property (ii), we may now invoke property (iii) to subtract $f+g$ from $D$:
   \[
        (D-(f+g))(x)=f(x)g(x)\in S.
   \]

Thus $fg$ belongs to $S$, completing the proof.

core_steps: Compose with ln(Â·+1) to put ln(f+1), ln(g+1) in S.
Add the two logs to get ln((f+1)(g+1)) in S.
Compose with the inverse expâˆ’1 map to obtain (f+1)(g+1)âˆ’1 = fg+f+g in S.
Add f and g to get f+g in S.
Subtract (fg+f+g)âˆ’(f+g) (non-negative) to place fg in S.

slot1.description: Base of the exponential / logarithm pair that are required to belong to S and be mutual inverses up to a fixed shift.

slot2.description: Fixed shift that keeps the argument of the logarithm positive and disappears after composing the inverse maps.

slot3.description: Lower end-point of the domain (and hence the non-negativity of every function value, needed for the order condition in (iii)).
